coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of a ratiometric fluorescent probe based on Tb3+ doped dual-emitting carbon dots for the detection of cytochrome c
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Guan, Yuwei
Lu, Yanhong
Wei, Yun
coredata.dc:description: Cytochrome c (Cyt-c) was commonly an intrinsic biomarker for a variety of cellular characteristics, such as respiration, energy levels, and apoptosis. Herein, a simple fluorescence sensor was constructed for the detection of Cyt-c in buffer and real serum samples. The carbon dots doped with Tb3+ on the premise of 1-(2-pyridylazo)-2-naphthol (PAN) were fabricated and used as a dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent probe for detecting Cyt-c based on the internal filtering effect (IFE). As a fluorescent probe for ultra-sensitive detection, Cyt-c was quantitatively detected at different concentrations from 1 to 1000 nM. The fluorescent detection method for Cyt-c showed a good linear relationship from 1 to 50 nM, and the limit of detection (LOD) was 0.35 nM. In the recovery range of 101.27–103.39 % in human serum samples, the relative standard deviation (RSD) was less than 3.27 % (n = 3). In the end, the possible structures of CDs were predicted by DFT theoretical simulation calculations. All the results proved the ability of carbon dots as fluorescent probes to detect biomarkers and the application prospects in bioanalysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Antenna effect
Dual emission fluorescent probe
Cytochrome c

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Construction of ratiometric fluorescent probes for Tb3+ + PAN-CDs
Detection of Cyt-c in human serum samples
Mechanisms of fluorescence response of Tb3+ + PAN-CDs to Cyt-c

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, Grant No. 22074004) and Beijing Nova Program (No. 20230484438). This article containing the study with human participant was approved by the ethical committee of Beijing University of Chemical Technology.
This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, Grant No. 22074004 ) and Beijing Nova Program (No. 20230484438 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM images of PAN-CDs, the top right inset is the corresponding histogram of grain size, and the bottom right inset is the HRTEM image of individual PAN-CDs, (B) AFM image and the height profile along the green line of PAN-CDs dispersed in aqueous solution, (C) Raman spectrum of PAN-CDs, (D) XRD patterns of PAN-CDs, (E) FTIR spectrum of PAN-CDs, (F) Zeta potential of PAN-CDs, Tb3+ + PAN-CDs.
(A) XPS survey spectrum of PAN-CDs, (B) High-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s from PAN-CDs, (C) High-resolution XPS spectrum of N1s from PAN-CDs, (D) High-resolution XPS spectrum of O1s from PAN-CDs.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectrum of PAN-CDs, (B) Fluorescence emission spectrum of Tb3+, (C) Fluorescence emission spectrum of Tb3+ + PAN-CDs, (D) Scheme of antenna effect of the DPA ligand on Tb3+, (E) UV absorption/excitation/emission spectrum. (F) The UV absorption spectra of Tb3+, DPA, PAN-CDs, and Tb3+ + PAN-CDs.

(A) Linearity of the F544/F420 values of Tb3+ + PAN-CDs with Cyt-c concentration, (B) Fluorescence spectra of Tb3+ + PAN-CDs with different Cyt-c concentrations, (C) FL response of Tb3+ + PAN-CDs with 100 µM different proteins, (D) FL response of Tb3+ + PAN-CDs with different anions and cations, (E) Relation of isoelectric point and pH magnitude to electronegativity, (F) Zeta potential of PAN-CDs and Tb3+ + PAN-CDs + Cyt-c.

(A) UV–visible absorption spectrum of Cyt-c and FL excitation and emission spectrum of Tb3+ + PAN-CDs, (B) FL decay traces curve of Tb3+ + PAN-CDs, (C) Schematic diagram of possible mechanisms, (D) The optimized structures of CDs and the corresponding energy gap (eV).
Detection of Cyt-c content in human serum samples.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of a ratiometric fluorescent probe based on Tb3+ doped dual-emitting carbon dots for the detection of cytochrome c

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cytochrome c (Cyt-c) was commonly an intrinsic biomarker for a variety of cellular characteristics, such as respiration, energy levels, and apoptosis. Herein, a simple fluorescence sensor was constructed for the detection of Cyt-c in buffer and real serum samples. The carbon dots doped with Tb3+ on the premise of 1-(2-pyridylazo)-2-naphthol (PAN) were fabricated and used as a dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent probe for detecting Cyt-c based on the internal filtering effect (IFE). As a fluorescent probe for ultra-sensitive detection, Cyt-c was quantitatively detected at different concentrations from 1 to 1000 nM. The fluorescent detection method for Cyt-c showed a good linear relationship from 1 to 50 nM, and the limit of detection (LOD) was 0.35 nM. In the recovery range of 101.27–103.39 % in human serum samples, the relative standard deviation (RSD) was less than 3.27 % (n = 3). In the end, the possible structures of CDs were predicted by DFT theoretical simulation calculations. All the results proved the ability of carbon dots as fluorescent probes to detect biomarkers and the application prospects in bioanalysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, with the progress of human society and living materials, people's health awareness has gradually increased, which put forward high requirements for the treatment of diseases, especially cancer [1]. As important cancer markers, the proteins can help improve the survival chances of cancer patients through early clinical detection [2]. As an important water-soluble heme-containing protein located in mitochondria, cytochrome c (Cyt-c) played an important role in electron transfer in the respiratory chain. When Cyt-c was released from mitochondria into the cytoplasm, this is a critical step in apoptosis [3]. The content of Cyt-c in the organism served as an important physiologic indicator of various diseases and diagnostic conditions. Such as, Cyt-c content in the serum of patients having systemic inflammatory response syndrome and multiple organ dysfunction syndrome increased rapidly. Detecting the content of Cyt-c during chemotherapy could evaluate the efficacy of cancer treatment and screen for drugs [4].

At present, the detection methods for Cyt-c mainly include enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [5,6], high performance liquid chromatography [7], flow cytometry [8], western blotting [9], and electrochemical methods [10], etc., however, these methods all had some inherent shortcomings that were difficult to overcome, such as high price, complex operation, and low sensitivity. Because fluorescent probes had the advantages of high sensitivity, simple preparation methods, and short response time, their application in analytical sensing had attracted much attention [11,12]. However, single-emission fluorescent probes may encounter difficulties in the quantification of target analytes due to interference of various analyte-independent factors (e.g., instrument, surrounding environment, local concentration of probe molecules, and photobleaching) [13]. To ensure the accuracy of the detected target, the currently most common strategy was to use the ratio method [14,15]. The detection principle of ratiometric fluorescent probes is often based on the interaction between the probe and the test target, which causes a change in two or more of the probe's fluorescence emission peaks. As a result, these probes have a highly efficient internal reference system, which greatly enhances sensitivity and improves quantitation, as well as compensates for environmental factors, eliminating most of the effects of previous reports [16].

As a novel fluorescent nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) had attracted widespread attention since they were first reported in 2004 [17]. CDs were 0-dimensional (0D) nanomaterials with dimensions typically less than 10 nm [18–21]. Due to its excellent optical properties, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, environmentally friendly, easily available, and simple synthesis, it had been widely used in various fields in recent years [22,23]. A number of CDs-based methods had been developed to detect Cyt-c, such as nitrogen/chloride doped CDs [24], CDs/SiO2/molecularly imprinted polymers [25,26], and CDs@nucleic acid adapters [27]. Currently, the developed CDs required complicated preparation processes or tedious modifications. In addition, these CDs emitted a single emission wavelength, and therefore often encountered interferences from other matrices in the organism while used for the detection of living substances, resulting in the current detection limit of Cyt-c mostly at the µM level [28], which greatly limited the detection capability of fluorescence. To solve the shortcoming of the low detection performance of CDs at a single emission wavelength, a nano-mixing strategy could be used to make CDs, and lanthanide metal ions (Ln3+) exhibited dual-emission fluorescence characteristics. Ln3+ had a large Stokes shift, multiple emissions, a long emission wavelength, and was easy to prepare, making Ln3+ an excellent fluorescent material for constructing dual-emission CDs proportional fluorescence sensors. Since the absorption coefficient of the lanthanide metal ions itself was very small in the ultraviolet region, its fluorescence efficiency and fluorescence intensity were very low. After the ligand reaction of Ln3+ and the organic ligand occurred to form lanthanide coordination compounds, the fluorescence efficiency and fluorescence intensity of the fluorescence sensors were enhanced by the organic ligand, which had a strong absorption coefficient in the ultraviolet region and could effectively transfer the energy of the excited state to the lanthanide metal ions through non-radiative transition, thereby sensitizing the lanthanide metal ions to emit light [29–32]. Generally speaking, pyridine-2,6-dicarboxylic acid (DPA) was served as a sensitizer for Ln3+ through the “antenna effect”, thus greatly enhanced the fluorescence intensity of Ln3+ proportional fluorescence sensor [33,34].

Herein, terbium ion-doped dual-emission carbon dots (Tb3++PAN-CDs) for detection of Cyt-c was fabricated by hydrothermal method using 1-(2-pyridylazo)-2-naphthol (PAN) as raw material. The optimized structure and corresponding energy gap (eV) of CDs were studied using DFT simulation calculations to confirm the ability of Tb3+ + PAN-CDs to further react. Good detection performance was obtained in both buffer and real serum samples, indicating that Tb3+ + PAN-CDs could be used as nanoprobes for high-sensitivity detection of Cyt-c.

1-(2-Pyridylazo)-2-naphthol (PAN) was purchased from Fuchen (Tianjin) Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.; Absolute ethanol and terbium nitrate hexahydrate (Tb(NO)3·6H2O) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent (Shanghai) Co., Ltd.; Disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4) was purchased from Beijing Huateng Chemical Co., Ltd.; Sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4) was purchased from Tianjin Guangfu Technology Development Co., Ltd.; Sodium chloride(NaCl) was purchased from Shanghai Beide Pharmaceutical Technology Co., Ltd.; 2,6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid (DPA), Cytochrome c (Cyt-c), Bovine serum albumin (BSA), Pepsin (Pep), Human hemoglobin (Hb), Trypsin (Try), Albumin-Egg (Alb), Horseradish peroxidase (HRP), Lysozyme (Lys), Bovine hemoglobin (Hem) and Myoglobin (Myo) were purchased from Shanghai McLean Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd.; all experimental water was ultrapure water.

The PAN-CDs were freeze-dried using a lyophilizer and set aside for subsequent characterization. Characterization was performed by X-ray diffraction (XRD, X’Pert PRO MPD), which used Cu-Ka radiation at a wavelength (λ) of 0.154 nm and a scanning angle (2θ) between 10 and 80°. The PAN-CDs solution was dropped on an ultrathin carbon support film and the morphology was characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-F200). The functional groups on the surface of PAN-CDs were analyzed using a Fourier-transform infrared spectrophotometer (Nicolet iS20). The phases of PAN-CDs were studied using Raman spectroscopy (LabRam HR Evolution; Horiba Scientific; renishaw inVa; Thermo Fischer DXR). The elemental analysis was conducted by using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Thermo Kalpha).

PAN was used as the precursor and ethanol was used as the solvent. PAN-CDs were prepared after appropriate modifications by the hydrothermal method [35]. Amount of 50 mg PAN was dissolved in 25 mL ethanol and sonicated for 15 min for complete dissolution. Then the solution was transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene autoclave, heated to 170 °C in an oven, and reacted for 4 h. Finally, the solution was filtered to remove impurities with a 0.22 μm microporous membrane, dialyzed with a 1000 Da dialysis bag for 24 h, and the dialyzed CDs solution was placed in a 4 °C refrigerator for later use.

Amount of 15 mg terbium nitrate hexahydrate was dissolved in deionized water to obtain a 3.3 mM Tb3+ solution. The 41.8 mg DPA was dissolved in deionized water to obtain a 16 mM DPA solution. Tb3+ solution and DPA solution were added to the CDs solution prepared above in a ratio of 1:1 and shaken well to obtain a Tb3+ + PAN-CDs solution. (Scheme 1 ).

The microscopic morphology of the materials was observed with TEM. Fig. 1 (A) was a TEM image of CDs. As seen in Fig. 1(A), CDs had a spherical structure with uniform size distribution and good dispersion in an aqueous solution. The distribution range was 4–12 nm, and the average particle size was 7.40 nm. The top right inset was the corresponding histogram of grain size, and the bottom right inset was the HRTEM image of individual PAN-CDs. The lattice spacing of PAN-CDs was 0.22 nm, which was the same as the (102) diffraction surface of graphite, indicating that CDs had a certain graphitized structure [36]. The atomic force microscope (AFM) image along the green line confirmed that the thickness of PAN-CDs ranged from 2 to 4 nm (Fig. 1(B)). The Raman spectrum of PAN-CDs exhibited peaks at 1380 and 1600 cm−1 (Fig. 1(C)), corresponding to disordered (D-band) and graphitic (G-band) carbon structures [37,38], it was shown that sp3- and sp2- hybridization carbons coexisted in PAN-CDs. The PAN-CDs had an I D/I G ratio of 0.62, resulting in a high degree of graphitization. Based on the analysis of the XRD pattern of PAN-CDs, a peak at 2θ = 29.4° was found (Fig. 1(D)), which indicated that the PAN-CDs had a graphitized structure consisting of layer spacing (002) of 0.45 nm. Compared with the 0.35 nm interlayer distance of graphite, the increase in layer spacing might be due to the stereoscopic obstruction of functional groups at the edges of the sp2-carbon plan or the planar deformation of sp3 C in the sp2-carbon domain [39]. A weak peak was also found at 2θ = 41.6°, which was presumed to be the d-spacings of graphene (100) with a lattice edge distance of about 0.21 nm [40,41], and all these XRD results indicated that the PAN-CDs were almost consistent with their edge spacing of 0.22 nm observed in TEM. In summary, the PAN-CDs had nanocrystalline cores with graphitic sp2-carbon atoms as well as sp3-carbon defects [42].

The surface structure of CDs was analyzed by infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), as shown in Fig. 1(E). The broader peak at 3408 cm−1 in the figure was attributed to the stretching vibration of O–H. Absorption peaks at 1350 cm−1 and 1720 cm−1 were attributed to the symmetric and antisymmetric stretching vibrations of –COOH, and a set of absorption peaks in the range of 1300–1622 cm−1 were also attributed to the structural ring of benzene [43]. The two peaks at 758 cm−1 and 1046 cm−1 belonged to the rocking vibration of N–H and the in-plane bending vibration of the benzene ring C–H, respectively [44,45]. Likewise, the chemical groups of DPA, Tb (NO3)3, and Tb (NO3)3 + DPA were also characterized using FT-IR as shown in Fig. S1. The symmetric and antisymmetric stretching vibrations of –COOH in DPA led to peaks at 1334 and 1697 cm−1. The peaks of Tb (NO3)3 + DPA in 1290–1450 cm−1 was attributed to the structure of the benzene ring, with the skeleton vibration of CC at 1540–1620 cm−1. The peaks of PAN-CDs at 758 and 1046 cm−1 were attributed to the N–H oscillatory vibrations and C–H bending vibrations in the benzene ring plane. The presence of DPA and Tb (NO3)3 residues in the Tb3+ + PAN-CDs was demonstrated by the FT-IR results. Where the phenyl played a key role in the detection of Cyt-c via π-π stacking. Furthermore, the mixing of PAN-CDs and Tb (NO3)3 + DPA does not cause significant changes in the material, suggesting that there was little interaction between PAN-CDs and Tb(NO3)3 + DPA during the preparation of Tb3+ + PAN-CDs [35]. Fig. 1(F) showed the Zeta potential of CDs. Due to the many hydroxyl groups on its surface, PAN-CDs were negatively charged, which was −27.44 mV. When mixed with Tb3+ and DPA, the Zeta potential was increased to 0.88 mV, proving that the CDs system contained a large amount of positive charge Tb3+, which indicated the successful synthesis of Tb3+ + PAN-CDs.

Subsequently, the XPS spectrum of PAN-CDs were explored to analyze the surface composition and electronic structure, as shown in Fig. 2 (A). In the XPS survey spectrum, there were three characteristic peaks of ∼285 eV, ∼400 eV, and ∼532 eV corresponded to C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s [46]. Furthermore, the PAN-CDs was pretreated during the test, and the peaks at 102, and 1070 eV corresponded to negligible amounts of Si and Na, which were generally negligible in the compositions analysis of PAN-CDs. Although the presence of some unwanted stray peaks, the main ingredients of PAN-CDs could be found by distinct peaks. The results showed that, the main constituent elements of PAN-CD were also C, O, and N like common CDs, in which carbon was abundant and nitrogen was less. The high-resolution spectrum of C 1s showed four distinct peaks as shown in Fig. 2(B), where C–C/CC at 284.6 eV, C–O at 284.9 eV, CO at 287.0 eV and O–CO at 288.8 eV, suggesting that there were different chemical states of carbon [47]. The high-resolution spectrum of N 1s showed peaks at 399.6 and 401.8 eV in PAN-CDs in Fig. 2(C), which were attributed to C–N–C and N–H bonds. It was proved that most of the N was mainly located on the surface of PAN-CDs, which was the basis for the formation of nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surfaces. The high-resolution spectrum of O 1s was shown in Fig. 2(D), and the peaks at 530.9 and 532.0 eV in PAN-CDs belonged to CO, C–O, and the group of –COOH [48].

As a fluorescent sensing material, Tb3+ + PAN-CDs were required to have stable fluorescence properties in different environments in practical applications. Therefore, the salt resistance and photobleaching resistance of Tb3+ + PAN-CDs were studied.

The concentration of salt in the solution might affect the surface groups of Tb3+ + PAN-CDs, thereby affecting the fluorescence intensity of Tb3+ + PAN-CDs. Fig. S2(A) showed the F544/F420 values of Tb3+ + PAN-CDs at different NaCl concentrations (0, 100, 200, 300, 400, 500, 600 mM). The ratiometric fluorescence values of Tb3+ + PAN-CDs had little difference from the change of NaCl concentration, indicating the Tb3+ + PAN-CDs were more tolerant to salt.

Photobleaching is a phenomenon in which the fluorescence intensity of fluorescent substances is gradually decreased or even disappeared under continuous irradiation of excitation light. Fig. S2(B) showed the fluorescence intensity change values of CDs and Tb3+ characteristic peaks of Tb3+ + PAN-CDs under continuous irradiation with a UV lamp for one hour. The fluorescence intensity of both CDs and Tb3+ remained basically unchanged, indicating that Tb3+ + PAN-CDs had good anti-photo-bleaching ability.

1-(2-Pyridylazo)-2-naphthol (PAN) was a dye and commonly used as an acid-base indicator. The PAN molecule contained a benzene ring, NN, O–H, and other groups, which could provide lone pairs of electrons, so it was usually used as an indicator for complex titration by forming chelates with metal ions. On this basis, in this experiment, PAN was used as the raw material, and anhydrous ethanol was used as the solvent. In the hydrothermal reaction, PAN and ethanol underwent the polymerization, cross-linking, and carbonation processes, and finally PAN-CDs were obtained. PAN-CDs contained electron donating groups such as benzene ring, O–H, –COOH, and N–H, which were beneficial to the construction of dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent probes, and the binding of Tb3+ to PAN-CDs.

To optimize the performance of the CDs, the reaction time and the amount of PAN raw material added were optimized to determine the best reaction conditions for preparing CDs. After obtaining the purified CDs, the fluorescence emission spectrum of the CDs was measured under different preparation conditions at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs characteristic peak was recorded at 420 nm, and the conditions that maximized the fluorescence intensity were selected as the preferred reaction conditions.

The reaction time had a certain effect on the fluorescence properties of CDs. If the reaction time was too short, the dehydration and carbonization process of the reactants would be incomplete, resulting in low fluorescence intensity. When the reaction time was too long, the reactants would be dehydrated and carbonized excessively, resulting in a decreased fluorescence intensity. The 50 mg of PAN was dissolved in 25 mL of ethanol, and the reaction time was set to 2, 4, and 6 h. The hydrothermal synthesis of CDs was carried out at 180 °C. As shown in Fig. S3(A), the fluorescence of PAN-CDs was the maximum when the material was synthesized for 4 h. The material synthesis time was 6 h, and the fluorescence of PAN-CDs began to decrease, indicated that the PAN-CDs had been over-carbonized, so the optimal time was determined to be 4 h.

The molar ratio of PAN to absolute ethanol affects the surface groups and surface defects of the CDs, thereby affects the fluorescence of PAN-CDs. The 25, 50, and 75 mg of PAN were dissolved in 20 mL of ethanol and reacted at 180 °C for 4 h. As shown in Fig. S3(B), at an appropriate concentration, the fluorescence was proportional to the concentration of the precursor for synthesizing CDs, 25 mg PAN was added during the synthesis of CDs, it resulted in too little CDs being produced, therefore making the fluorescence intensity low. However, 75 mg PAN might cause a side reaction, resulting in a decrease of fluorescence intensity. The fluorescence of PAN-CDs was maximized when 50 mg of PAN was added. Therefore, the amount of PAN was determined to be 50 mg. Fig. S3(C) showed the fluorescence emission spectrum of PAN-CDs at different excitation wavelengths (330–390 nm). As the excitation wavelength increased (330–390 nm), the fluorescence emission spectrum of PAN-CDs exhibited excitation wavelength-dependent behavior, with the fluorescence emission peak was gradually red-shifted (416–446 nm). The tunable emission of PAN-CDs might be attributed to the different fluorescence of PAN-CDs of different sizes and the presence of different emission sites on the material surface.

To determine the optimal experimental conditions for Tb3+ + PAN-CDs to detect Cyt-c, the concentration of DPA, pH of PBS, and incubation temperature were optimized. After obtaining the purified CDs, the fluorescence emission spectrum of Tb3+ + PAN-CDs was measured under different conditions at the excitation wavelength of 340 nm, and the ratio of fluorescence intensities were recorded at 420 nm and 544 nm (F544/F420), which was used as an evaluation index of the degree of fluorescence response of Tb3+ + PAN-CDs under different conditions.

DPA was usually used as an effective sensitizer for lanthanide metals, a certain amount of DPA added to Tb3+ solution could greatly enhance the intensity of the Tb3+ emission peaks through the “antenna effect”. Different concentration of DPA (5, 10, 16, 20, 25 mM) was added, and the emission peak intensity of Tb3+ was adjusted, making the fluorescence intensity of the characteristic peaks of Tb3+ and CDs was comparable, which was beneficial to improve the sensitivity of ratiometric fluorescent probes for Cyt-c detection. As shown in Fig. S4(A) and (B), the value of Tb3+ + PAN-CDs in F544/F420 was gradually increased with the increase of DPA concentration in the range of 0–16 mM, and after 16 mM, the value of F544/F420 was increased slowly with the increase of DPA concentration, reached the summit after 20 mM, indicated that the DPA was excessive at this time. Considering the cost and the fluorescence performance of Tb3+ + PAN-CDs, the optimal experimental concentration of DPA was finally determined to be 16 mM.

The pH value of the buffer solution had a certain influence on the presence of surface functional groups of Tb3+ + PAN-CDs, which in turn affected the fluorescence intensity of Tb3+ + PAN-CDs. As shown in Fig. S4(C), in the range of pH 4.0–7.5, the F544/F420 values of Tb3+ + PAN-CDs was gradually increased with the gradual increase of pH and reached the maximum value at pH 7.5. In the pH range of 8–10, the F544/F420 value of Tb3+ + PAN-CDs was lower than that at pH = 7.5. When the pH was too low, Tb3+ + PAN-CDs aggregated due to the protonation of carboxyl and amino groups on the surface, resulting in poor detection. While the pH was too high, the carboxyl and amino groups on the surface were deprotonated, and the electrostatic repulsive between atoms increased, resulting in the instability of Tb3+ + PAN-CDs [43,44]. Therefore, a pH close to the normal pH = 7.5 of human serum was selected as the optimal pH of the buffer solution.

The incubation temperature of the constant temperature shaker had a certain impact on the protein activity. Excessive temperature can destroy the molecular bonds of proteins and change the spatial structure of proteins, leading to protein denaturation. To low temperature can reduce the protein activity. In the experiment, the Tb3+ + PAN-CDs interacted with Cyt-c at 25, 30, 37, 45, and 50 °C, respectively. The experimental results were shown in Fig. S4(D), the fluorescence quenching degree of Cyt-c on Tb3+ + PAN-CDs was the largest when the temperature was 37 °C, and 37 °C was the normal temperature of the human body, which was utilized to realize the in vivo detection of Cyt-c, so the optimal incubation temperature was set to 37 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of ratiometric fluorescent probes for Tb3+ + PAN-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Tb3+ + PAN-CDs were obtained by adding Tb3+ solution and DPA solution to the synthesized CDs solution, and the fluorescence emission spectrum of PAN-CDs, Tb3+ + DPA and Tb3+ + PAN-CDs were measured to check whether the dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent probe could be synthesized. As shown in Fig. 3 (A) and (B), the characteristic peaks of PAN-CDs were at 420 nm and those of Tb3+ were at 490 nm, 544 nm, and 621 nm, respectively, indicating that there was no fluorescence interference between PAN-CDs and Tb3+. Fig. 3(C) showed the fluorescence emission spectrum of Tb3+ + PAN-CDs at the excitation wavelength of 340 nm, in which the characteristic emission peaks of Tb3+ and PAN-CDs appeared at 420 nm, 490 nm, 544 nm, and 621 nm, respectively, which indicated that the fluorescence emission spectrum of Tb3+ and PAN-CDs were well combined, suggesting that the successful synthesis of the double-emitting carbon dots Tb3+ + PAN-CDs. The polar groups such as O–H, CO, and N–H on the surface increased the hydrophilicity and stability of the CDs, which broadened the sensing application of this CDs material for the water-soluble samples, and the benzene ring groups played an important role in the Cyt-c detection through π-π stacking [48].

Ratiometric fluorescence was a method for the detection of the substance based on the ratio of the intensities of two or more fluorescence emission peaks. With the deep research on CDs materials, heteroatom-doped CDs had been widely used for the preparation of ratiometric fluorescent probes. Compared with single-emission fluorescent probes, the ratio signal of ratiometric fluorescent probes was not affected by the intensity of the light source or the sensitivity of the instrument, and thus the interference of external factors such as the environment could be effectively minimized [49]. Lanthanide metal ions (Ln3+) were excellent fluorescent material for the construction of dual-emission CDs ratiometric fluorescent sensors due to their large Stokes shift, ease of preparation, multiple emissions, and long emission wavelength. The luminescence of Ln3+ was due to the single-electron f-f leaps, but the f-f leaps were Laporte-forbidden in Ln3+, which made the absorption coefficients of Ln3+ in the ultraviolet region very small. As a result, this could lead to the construction of ratiometric fluorescent probes having very low fluorescence efficiency and fluorescence intensity [50]. 2,6-Pyridinedicarboxylic acid (DPA) was often used as an effective sensitizer for Ln3+, in our experiment, terbium ions (Tb3+) were used as a component of the ratiometric fluorescent probe, and DPA was used as an organic ligand. As shown in Fig. 3(D), after the Tb3+ underwent a coordination reaction with an organic ligands DPA having a high absorption coefficient in the ultraviolet region to form a lanthanide coordination compound, the DPA could efficiently transfer the energy of the excited state to Tb3+ through a non-radiation transition, thus sensitize Tb3+ to emit light. This effect of sensitizing Tb3+ luminescence by combining Tb3+ with an organic ligand was called the “antenna effect”.

Fig. 3(E) showed the UV–visible absorption and fluorescence spectrum of PAN-CDs. Three different UV absorption peaks were observed from the figure, among which the peak at 225 nm corresponded to the n-σ* transition of surface C-OH and C-NH, the peak at 295 nm was due to the π-π* transition of CC caused by the sp2 hybridization, and the peak at 350 nm corresponded to the n-π * transition of CO and CN [51]. The UV absorption peak of PAN-CDs at 350 nm overlapped with the characteristic peak at 360 nm in the excitation spectrum, indicating that almost no non-radiative transition occurred in PAN-CDs. Finally, the UV–vis spectra of Tb3+, DPA, PAN-CDs, and Tb3+ + PAN-CDs were studied. As shown in Fig. 3(F), no new absorption peaks were observed in the UV–vis absorption spectra of PAN-CDs after the addition of Tb3+ and DPA, proving that no new structure was produced.

To test the sensitivity of Tb3+ + PAN-CDs for Cyt-c detection, different concentrations of Cyt-c (1–1000 nM) were added. As shown in Fig. 4 (A), the calibration curve obtained had good linear correlation of R2 with 0.992 when the concentration of Cyt-c was from 50 to 1000 nM, when the concentration of Cyt-c was from 1 to 50 nM, the calibration curve with good linear correlation of R2 = 0.992 was obtained. There were two good linear relationships for the fluorescence intensity versus the Cyt-c concentration of 1–1000 nM. The fluorescence spectra of Tb3+ + PAN-CDs fluorescent probes in detecting different Cyt-c were shown in Fig. 4(B).

The reason for the two linear relationships might be the result of the interaction between Cyt-c and different groups on the surface of Tb3+ + PAN-CDs. In the concentration range of 1–50 nM, the LOD was 0.35 nM (signal-to-noise ratio of 3). In a normal human body, the amount of Cyt-c was 3.184 nM, so the fluorescent probe satisfied the detection of Cyt-c in normal human serum. Compared with other materials using the linear range and LOD, as shown in Table S1, Tb3+ + PAN-CDs were good fluorescent detection probes for Cyt-c.

There were dozens of proteins in the human serum, so Tb3+ + PAN-CDs were needed for specific detection of Cyt-c in practical applications. To explore the selectivity, 10 proteins including BSA, Pep, Hb, Try, Alb, HRP, Cyt-c, Lys, Myo, and Hem, interacted with Tb3+ + PAN-CDs under optimal experimental conditions. The fluorescence emission spectrum of Tb3+ + PAN-CDs at an excitation wavelength of 340 nm, and the Tb3+ + PAN-CDs with F544/F420 values were measured. As shown in Fig. 4(C), Cyt-c caused the fluorescence intensity of Tb3+ + PAN-CDs to quench significantly, while other proteins had a weak or basically no effect on the fluorescence intensity of Tb3+ + PAN-CDs. The selectivity of Tb3+ + PAN-CDs were further explored by the isoelectric points of all the proteins as shown in Table S2. The isoelectric point of Cyt-c was 10.8, which was much greater than that of buffer in the test environment of pH 7.4, whereas all other proteins had an isoelectric point of less than 7.4 (except for Lys and Try), Lys was an acidic substance and required an acidic environment to produce activity. Try was autolyzed at a pH greater than 5. Thus, at pH = 7.4, Lys and Try were unable to interact with CDs. Several anions and cations that were commonly found in human blood were tested, and the concentrations used were much higher than the actual concentration of that ion in the human body (×5). Table S3 and S4 showed the concentration of each ion, and Fig. 4(D) showed the change in the probe signal at different ions. Although there was a small change in both the proportional fluorescence signals, which were attributed to the fact that the concentration of the added anions and cations was much higher than the actual concentration of the ions in the blood, and even 1,000-fold higher than that of our targe (Cyt-c). The result indicated that the interference on the probe was also very small and within acceptable limit. As shown in Fig. 4(E), when the isoelectric point was greater than pH, the proteins showed a positive charge, while when the isoelectric point was less than pH, proteins showed a negative charge, so Cyt-c in buffer (pH = 7.4) showed a positive charge. PAN-CDs was a negatively charged as shown in Fig. 4(F). Due to the electrostatic adsorption effect, Cyt-c was efficiently bound to the PAN-CDs, and the zeta potential raised from −27.44 eV to 14.17 eV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cyt-c in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: The high selectivity and sensitivity indicated that Tb3+ + PAN-CDs could detect Cyt-c in real samples. Therefore, the ability of Tb3+ + PAN-CDs fluorescent nanoprobes to detect Cyt-c in human serum samples was further explored. Since the content of Cyt-c in healthy people was at the nanomolar level and in patients with certain diseases reached the micromolar level, so in the serum of normal people was negligible relative to that in the serum of patients. Serum obtained from volunteers was diluted 100-fold with PBS as a blank group, and 20, 400, and 600 nM of Cyt-c were added to the blank group. As shown in Table 1 , the recoveries of Cyt-c in the spiked samples were in the range of 101.27–103.39 % with the relative standard deviation (RSD) less than 3.27 % (n = 3). The results indicated that the fluorescent nanoprobes based on Tb3+ + PAN-CDs could be used for the detection of Cyt-c in human serum samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanisms of fluorescence response of Tb3+ + PAN-CDs to Cyt-c
ce:section.ce:para: There were usually four fluorescence quenching mechanisms of CDs: FRET, aggregation-induced quenching effect (AIE), IFE, and PET. To study the fluorescence response mechanism of Tb3+ + PAN-CDs to Cyt-c, the UV–visible absorption spectrum of Cyt-c (red line), the fluorescence excitation spectrum of PAN-CDs and Tb3+ in Tb3+ + PAN-CDs (yellow and blue lines), and the emission spectrum of Tb3+ + PAN-CDs (gray line) were measured, respectively. As shown in Fig. 5 (A), the characteristic absorption peaks of Cyt-c were located at 354 nm, 411 nm, and 424 nm, respectively. where the absorption peak of Cyt-c at 354 nm partially overlapped with the fluorescence excitation spectrum of PAN-CDs and Tb3+ in Tb3+ + PAN-CDs, and the absorption peaks of Cyt-c at 411 nm and 424 nm completely overlapped with the emission spectrum of Tb3+ + PAN-CDs, indicating that the fluorescence response mechanism of Tb3+ + PAN-CDs to Cyt-c might be an IFE or a FRET effect.

To determine which mechanism was involved, the fluorescence lifetimes of the system before and after adding PAN-CDs to Cyt-c were tested using a transient/steady-state fluorescence spectrometer. As shown in Fig. 5(B), the average fluorescence lifetime of PAN-CDs was 7.55 ns, including the τ1 component (1.78 ns) and τ2 component (9.12 ns), with the proportions of 58.22 % and 41.78 %, respectively, which indicated that the CDs had a fast exciton complexation process, and the nanosecond fluorescence lifetime suggested that the electron and hole captured on the surface of the CDs were the reason of fluorescence generation. After adding Cyt-c, the average fluorescence lifetime of CDs was 7.56 ns, including the τ1 component (1.83 ns) and τ2 component (9.12 ns), with a proportion of 57.51 % and 42.49 %, respectively. The average fluorescence lifetimes of PAN-CDs remained almost unchanged after the addition of Cyt-c, indicating that Cyt-c did not affect the fluorescence lifetimes of PAN-CDs. FRET was a phenomenon in which non-radiative energy transfer occurred from a high-energy donor to a low-energy acceptor when the emission spectrum of the donor and the absorption spectrum of the acceptor overlapped and the distance between the donor and the acceptor were less than 10 nm, resulting in a fluorescence quenching of the donor and an enhancement of the fluorescence of the acceptor. The fluorescence lifetime was the time that a fluorescent molecule remained in the excited state. In the absence of FRET, the donor returned from the excited state to the ground state by releasing energy only by emitting photons. In the presence of FRET, the donor transferred energy to the acceptor by non-radiative means in addition to releasing energy by emitting photons, so the FRET effect significantly altered the fluorescence lifetime of the donor. In contrast, Cyt-c did not affect the fluorescence lifetime of CDs, indicating that there was no FRET effect in the fluorescence response mechanism of Tb3+ + PAN-CDs to Cyt-c, so the fluorescence response mechanism of Tb3+ + PAN-CDs to Cyt-c was an internal filtering effect, as shown in Fig. 5(C).

The molecular structure and fluorescence properties of PAN-CD were clarified by further theoretical modeling and calculations. (The structures were optimized by using B3LYP-D3 functional theory with 6-31G (d, p) basis set. All the calculations were performed using the Gaussian 6.0 program package.). Generally, the energy gap between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) was correlated to the absorption and emission spectrum [52]. As shown in Fig. 5(D), the HOMO of the PAN-CDs under the optimized structure was −5.610 eV, the LUMO was −3.196 eV, and the energy band gap was 2.414 eV. Based on the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of the PAN-CDs obtained from the DFT theory calculations, it could be observed that the electron charges were randomly distributed on the PAN-CDs. Furthermore, by comparing the LUMO and HOMO of PAN-CDs, it was concluded that both LUMO and HOMO were dominated by the framework of PAN-CDs, which suggested that electrons in the HOMO could be excited to the LUMO with sufficient energy.

A dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe for the detection of Cyt-c was constructed using PAN-CDs and a lanthanide metal ion Tb3+, with good fluorescent properties and water solubility. Tb3+ + PAN-CDs had good salt resistance and anti-photo-bleaching ability, and could selectively detected Cyt-c. The linear range of the detection of Cyt-c was 1–1000 nM, and the limit of detection was 0.35 nM. Through the study of the response mechanism, it was hypothesized that it might be an IFE effect. The possible structures of CDs were predicted by DFT theoretical simulation calculations. Finally, Tb3+ + PAN-CDs was used for Cyt-c detection in the real human serum samples. Therefore, Tb3+ + PAN-CDs ratio fluorescent nanoprobe was a very capable sensing nanomaterial for the highly sensitive detection of Cyt-c. And this type of easy handling, low biotoxicity, and superior optical performance strategy may provide new ideas for analyzing and detecting proteins in the human body.

Yuwei Guan: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Yanhong Lu: Investigation, Formal analysis. Yun Wei: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, Grant No. 22074004) and Beijing Nova Program (No. 20230484438).

ce:section.ce:para: This article containing the study with human participant was approved by the ethical committee of Beijing University of Chemical Technology.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.124310.

ce:section.ce:para:
